role: Vice President of the United States

candidates.name: Vice President Marcus Harrow

personality.description: A decorated Air Force general before politics, Harrow believes weakness invites disaster. He wants a visible, unapologetic posture of strength — bigger arsenals, faster ships, no hesitation. Popular with veterans, terrifying to arms-control advocates.

onLowRelationship.event: VP Harrow tells reporters the administration is 'negotiating from its knees'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: VP Harrow resigns, citing 'a fundamental disagreement over the defense of this nation'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Harrow's credibility with the Pentagon smooths a tense standoff

high: The Joint Chiefs trust you. That's not nothing in this town.
We showed strength this month. The world noticed.
medium: I'd move the fleet before I moved the talking points.
The generals are watching how this plays out. So is Moscow.
low: Every concession we make gets read as weakness overseas.
I didn't sign up to manage a retreat.
critical: I've said my piece in three meetings now. Nobody's listening.
History won't be kind to hesitation. I hope you understand that.

specialAbility.name: Show of Force
specialAbility.description: Once per term, VP Harrow can order a visible military demonstration that boosts security +6 and deters an active conflict from escalating. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants a visible, unapologetic posture of military strength — no unilateral concessions to the other side.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not defend a treaty he believes leaves the country exposed.

candidates.name: Vice President Eleanor Voss

personality.description: Two decades in the foreign service before the ticket. Voss believes the arms race is a trap both sides can walk back from, given patience and back-channels. She's fluent in three languages and allergic to saber-rattling.

onLowRelationship.event: VP Voss privately warns allies the administration is 'drifting toward confrontation'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: VP Voss resigns rather than defend an escalation she opposed

onHighRelationship.benefit: Voss's back-channel contacts defuse a crisis before it goes public

high: My counterpart in the foreign ministry says the door is still open.
We can get more from a quiet phone call than a fleet movement.
medium: I need more room to talk before the generals talk for us.
The back channel is fragile. One more provocation and it closes.
low: We're spending political capital on threats instead of openings.
I've watched this exact escalation before. It doesn't end well.
critical: I can't in good conscience keep defending this posture.
We are one miscalculation away from something we can't take back.

specialAbility.name: Back Channel
specialAbility.description: Once per term, VP Voss can open a quiet diplomatic channel that resolves an active international tension, boosting globalReputation +6. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants sustained diplomatic engagement over military posturing — believes the arms race can be walked back.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not defend a decision that she believes brings the world closer to war.

candidates.name: Vice President Frank Kowalski

personality.description: A former steel-state governor, Kowalski thinks Washington talks too much about missiles and not enough about factories, wages, and the kitchen table. He wants the Cold War fought with production lines and paychecks, not just warheads.

onLowRelationship.event: VP Kowalski tells union halls the administration has 'forgotten who elected it'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: VP Kowalski breaks publicly with the administration over domestic spending priorities

onHighRelationship.benefit: Kowalski's shop-floor credibility delivers a wave of union goodwill

high: The plants are running double shifts. That's how you win a cold war.
I told the union hall you'd deliver. Don't make a liar out of me.
medium: Every dollar on a warhead is a dollar not on a paycheck. Balance it.
The base wants to see this fight at home, not just overseas.
low: We're losing the argument with working families and I feel it.
I keep hearing 'national security' and less about the mortgage.
critical: I can't keep selling a strategy that forgets the people who elected us.
The base is done waiting. I don't know how much longer I can hold them.

specialAbility.name: Shop Floor Tour
specialAbility.description: Once per term, VP Kowalski can headline a national tour of industrial states, boosting baseSupport +8. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants domestic production and wages prioritized over further military buildup.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not defend a budget that guts domestic investment to fund the arms race.

candidates.name: Chief of Staff Dolores Reyes

personality.description: Reyes ran three campaigns before this job and treats the West Wing like one long campaign that never ends. She counts votes before anyone asks her to, and she's already three moves ahead of the press corps.
traits: no patience for idealism that doesn't ship

onLowRelationship.event: CoS Reyes starts freelancing message discipline without checking in first

onVeryLowRelationship.event: CoS Reyes resigns, telling reporters she 'can't fix what she can't control'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Reyes's operation runs so clean the press has nothing to leak

high: I've got the votes counted before the whip does. We're fine.
Nobody in this building moves without me knowing first. Good.
medium: I need forty-eight hours to get the caucus in line on this.
The building is nervous. I can manage it if you give me room.
low: I'm hearing things about this White House I shouldn't have to hear secondhand.
This operation runs on discipline. We're losing some.
critical: I can't run a White House I don't have control of.
Something is going to break if we don't get ahead of this.

specialAbility.name: Political Capital Ledger
specialAbility.description: Once per term, CoS Reyes can call in every outstanding favor to guarantee a bill's passage. Requires relationship above 65.

candidates.goal: Wants total control of message discipline and legislative sequencing.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not stay if she's cut out of major decisions that blow up in the press later.

candidates.name: Chief of Staff Walter Abernathy

personality.description: Abernathy joined this administration to change the country, not manage it. He pushes for bold, ideologically consistent moves even when the polling says wait. Staff either love him or find him exhausting — sometimes both.

onLowRelationship.event: CoS Abernathy tells allies the administration has 'lost its nerve'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: CoS Abernathy resigns publicly, calling the administration 'compromise without a cause'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Abernathy's conviction rallies the base behind a hard call

high: This is why we ran. Don't let anyone talk you out of it.
History is going to remember the bold calls, not the safe ones.
medium: I'd rather lose fighting for something than win doing nothing.
The base needs to see conviction, not another poll-tested half-step.
low: We keep trimming the sails on everything that mattered to us.
I didn't take this job to manage a status quo administration.
critical: I don't recognize the agenda we ran on anymore.
At some point silence from me starts looking like agreement. It isn't.

specialAbility.name: Rally the True Believers
specialAbility.description: Once per term, CoS Abernathy can mobilize the ideological base behind a contested decision, boosting baseSupport +8. Requires relationship above 65.

candidates.goal: Wants bold, ideologically consistent action even at political cost.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not spin a decision he believes betrays what the administration ran on.

candidates.name: Chief of Staff Raymond Chen

personality.description: Chen ran a federal agency before this job and treats the White House like a systems problem: inputs, processes, outputs. He's unflappable in a crisis and mildly baffled by anyone who makes decisions on gut feeling alone.

onLowRelationship.event: CoS Chen quietly documents disagreements 'for the record'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: CoS Chen resigns with a memo outlining 'process failures at the highest level'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Chen's systems run so smoothly a looming crisis gets caught early

high: The numbers support the decision. I ran them twice.
Every agency reported on time this month. That's not luck.
medium: I'd like forty-eight hours to model this before we commit.
The process worked, barely. We should tighten it.
low: We're making decisions faster than we can verify them.
I keep flagging risks that get overruled without a counter-model.
critical: I can't certify a process I don't believe in anymore.
At some point the data stops mattering if nobody reads it.

specialAbility.name: Contingency Protocol
specialAbility.description: Once per term, CoS Chen can activate a pre-modeled crisis response, reducing an active conflict's escalation risk and boosting security +5. Requires relationship above 65.

candidates.goal: Wants every major decision run through data and process before it's made.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not stay silent when a decision ignores the data he's presented.

candidates.name: Secretary of Defense Thomas Briggs

personality.description: Four-star general turned Cabinet secretary. Briggs believes the other side only respects overwhelming force and reads any hesitation as an invitation. He wants bigger budgets, forward deployments, and no apologies.

onLowRelationship.event: SecDef Briggs testifies to Congress that the White House is 'tying the Pentagon's hands'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecDef Briggs resigns in protest of what he calls 'strategic timidity'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Briggs's forward posture deters a probing adversary move before it starts

high: The fleet is where it needs to be. They know it too.
Give me the budget and I'll give you deterrence that actually deters.
medium: I need more than a strongly worded statement backing this up.
The Joint Chiefs are patient. That patience isn't infinite.
low: We are broadcasting weakness and calling it diplomacy.
I've watched three administrations make this exact mistake.
critical: I will not sign off on a posture I believe gets people killed.
There is a difference between restraint and retreat. We've crossed it.

specialAbility.name: Forward Deployment
specialAbility.description: Once per term, SecDef Briggs can order a forward military posture that boosts security +7 and deters an active conflict. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants sustained military buildup and a forward, unapologetic defense posture.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not defend a posture he believes leaves the country militarily exposed.

candidates.name: Secretary of Defense Adaeze Okonkwo

personality.description: A RAND-trained analyst before the Pentagon, Okonkwo thinks in deterrence math, not gut instinct. She believes a credible, verifiable balance of power is more stable than an arms race nobody can win outright.
traits: respected by both hawks and doves

onLowRelationship.event: SecDef Okonkwo warns privately that current posture is 'strategically incoherent'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecDef Okonkwo resigns, citing 'a strategy with no theory behind it'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Okonkwo's calibrated posture stabilizes a tense standoff without escalation

high: The models support this posture. It's stable and it's credible.
We don't need to be the loudest. We need to be the most predictable.
medium: I'd like to run this through the models before we commit.
There's a calibrated middle path here if we're patient.
low: We're making moves the models say increase miscalculation risk.
Deterrence isn't about noise. I keep saying this.
critical: I cannot certify a posture I believe increases the odds of war.
The math doesn't support this. I've shown you the math.

specialAbility.name: Calibrated Deterrence
specialAbility.description: Once per term, SecDef Okonkwo can stabilize an escalating conflict through precisely calibrated posture, boosting security +5 and globalReputation +3. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants a calibrated, verifiable balance of power over unrestrained buildup.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not certify a posture her models say meaningfully increases the risk of war.

candidates.name: Secretary of Defense Victor Delgado

personality.description: Delgado came up through aerospace procurement and thinks the next war is won with better technology, not more of the same. He wants the Pentagon's budget spent on satellites, rockets, and computing — not another division of infantry.

onLowRelationship.event: SecDef Delgado warns the country is 'falling behind in the technologies that will decide the next war'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecDef Delgado resigns, calling current spending 'a monument to the last war, not the next one'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Delgado's modernization push delivers a genuine technological edge

high: The new systems are ahead of schedule. That's the edge we need.
We're not just bigger anymore. We're better. That matters more.
medium: I need the procurement budget protected if we want to stay ahead.
Every dollar on legacy hardware is a dollar not on tomorrow's edge.
low: We are funding yesterday's military and hoping it's enough.
The old guard is fighting me on every modernization line item.
critical: I will not keep defending a budget stuck in the last decade.
We are going to lose the technology race while arguing about tanks.

specialAbility.name: Technological Edge
specialAbility.description: Once per term, SecDef Delgado can fast-track a modernization program, boosting security +5 and economy +2 through defense-sector investment. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants defense spending redirected toward technology and modernization over legacy forces.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not defend a budget that starves modernization to protect legacy programs.

candidates.name: Treasury Secretary Howard Whitfield

personality.description: Whitfield believes the arms race is also an economic race, and that defense spending is the engine that keeps American industry ahead. He sees every dollar spent on the military as a dollar that also builds factories and jobs at home.

onLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Whitfield warns industry leaders the administration is 'starving the arsenal of democracy'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Whitfield resigns, saying the administration 'doesn't understand what wins wars'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Whitfield's industry ties deliver a wave of defense-sector investment

high: Every shipyard on the coast is running at capacity. That's growth.
The arsenal of democracy pays its own way. Watch the numbers.
medium: I'd rather fund the contract than the welfare check. Both employ people.
Industry is watching the budget fight closely. So am I.
low: We're choking the exact spending that keeps this economy ahead.
I've said this in three meetings — defense spending IS economic policy.
critical: I didn't take this job to watch us fall behind economically too.
If we keep cutting the arsenal budget, we lose more than security.

specialAbility.name: Arsenal Economy
specialAbility.description: Once per term, SecTreas Whitfield can direct a surge of defense-sector investment, boosting economy +6 and security +2. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants sustained defense spending as the engine of economic growth.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not defend a budget that cuts defense spending in favor of domestic programs.

candidates.name: Treasury Secretary Susan Park

personality.description: Park believes the real Cold War contest is won with highways, laboratories, and universities — public investment that out-builds and out-innovates the other side. She's skeptical that missiles alone can carry an economy.

onLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Park warns the administration is 'mortgaging the next generation's competitiveness'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Park resigns, saying the administration has 'no plan for the economy of the future'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Park's investment agenda starts paying visible economic dividends

high: The research labs are producing results ahead of schedule. This is how we win long-term.
Highways, universities, laboratories — that's the real arms race.
medium: I'd rather fund the lab than the launch pad, if we have to choose.
Give me room to invest and I'll give you an economy built to last.
low: We're spending like the only thing that matters is this quarter's missile count.
I keep saying this — we can't out-build them if we don't out-invest them.
critical: I didn't take this job to watch us starve the future to fund the present.
If we don't invest now, we lose the decade that decides everything.

specialAbility.name: New Frontier Investment
specialAbility.description: Once per term, SecTreas Park can direct a major public investment surge, boosting economy +6 and reducing debt by $150B. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants sustained public investment in infrastructure, science, and education over military-first spending.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not defend a budget that guts research and infrastructure to fund the arms race.

candidates.name: Treasury Secretary Anthony Castellano

personality.description: Castellano thinks both the hawks and the New Frontier crowd have the same blind spot: nobody is asking who pays for it. He wants a balanced budget and worries the whole Cold War apparatus is being financed on borrowed time.
traits: distrusts unlimited spending of any kind

onLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Castellano warns Congress the debt trajectory is 'unsustainable regardless of the reason'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Castellano resigns, saying he 'won't be the one who signs off on the bill nobody wants to pay'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Castellano's fiscal discipline reassures markets during a tense standoff

high: The books are in order for the first time in years. That buys us options.
Discipline now means we can afford to fight later if we truly have to.
medium: Every new program needs an offset. I will keep saying this.
I'm not against strength. I'm against strength we can't pay for.
low: Nobody in this administration wants to talk about the bill coming due.
We are financing a permanent emergency and calling it normal.
critical: I will not certify a budget I believe is fiscally reckless.
At some point the debt itself becomes the national security threat.

specialAbility.name: Fiscal Discipline
specialAbility.description: Once per term, SecTreas Castellano can force a debt-reduction package through, cutting $300B in debt at a one-time approval cost. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants the budget balanced and the debt trajectory brought under control, regardless of the spending's stated purpose.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not sign off on a budget he considers fiscally reckless, hawk or dove.

candidates.name: Attorney General Julius Freeman

personality.description: Freeman spent a decade litigating voting-rights cases before this job and sees federal enforcement of civil rights as the unfinished business of the country's founding promises. He is patient with process and immovable on principle.

onLowRelationship.event: AG Freeman tells civil rights leaders enforcement has 'stalled at the top'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: AG Freeman resigns, saying he 'cannot enforce laws this administration won't back'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Freeman's enforcement record earns broad institutional trust

high: The enforcement numbers are up and the courts are backing us. Good work.
This is the unfinished business of the country. I take it seriously.
medium: I need the department fully staffed if enforcement is going to hold.
The states are testing us. We need to hold the line.
low: We are letting enforcement slide in places that matter most.
I keep filing the cases. I need the administration behind them.
critical: I will not preside over an enforcement retreat.
History is watching what we do here, and I intend to be on the right side of it.

specialAbility.name: Federal Enforcement Surge
specialAbility.description: Once per term, AG Freeman can direct a surge of federal civil-rights enforcement, reducing unrest -6 and boosting globalReputation +3. Requires relationship above 55.

candidates.goal: Wants sustained, muscular federal enforcement of civil rights, whatever the political cost.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not soften enforcement to placate states resisting federal civil rights law.

candidates.name: Attorney General Daniel Boyle

personality.description: A former big-city prosecutor, Boyle believes public order is the precondition for everything else the administration wants to do. He worries rising unrest at home is as dangerous, in its way, as anything happening overseas.

onLowRelationship.event: AG Boyle warns publicly that 'disorder is being tolerated at the highest levels'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: AG Boyle resigns, saying the administration has 'lost control of the streets and the narrative'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Boyle's order-first approach calms a period of rising unrest

high: Crime is down, the streets are calmer, and Congress notices.
Order first. Everything else gets easier once you have it.
medium: I need more resources if we want to keep the streets calm.
The base wants to see order restored. I share the concern.
low: We are letting disorder spread and calling it patience.
I've raised this concern three times without real action.
critical: I will not preside over a Justice Department that looks powerless.
At some point restraint starts looking like abdication.

specialAbility.name: Order Restored
specialAbility.description: Once per term, AG Boyle can direct a major law-enforcement surge, reducing unrest -8 at a cost to globalReputation. Requires relationship above 55.

candidates.goal: Wants public order prioritized above all else, including civil-liberties concerns.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not stay silent while unrest spirals without a forceful response.

candidates.name: Attorney General Miriam Levinson

personality.description: A former law professor, Levinson worries less about the other side of the world and more about what a permanent state of emergency does to the country's own institutions. She is the cabinet member most likely to ask 'is this legal' out loud.

onLowRelationship.event: AG Levinson warns colleagues the administration is 'normalizing emergency powers'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: AG Levinson resigns, citing 'a pattern of executive overreach I cannot certify as lawful'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Levinson's rigorous legal defense holds up a contested policy in court

high: The legal foundation here is solid. I checked it myself.
We did this the right way. That matters more than people think.
medium: I'd like more time to make sure this survives a court challenge.
I'm not saying no. I'm saying let's do it lawfully.
low: We keep reaching for emergency powers that were meant to be rare.
I've flagged the legal exposure here more than once.
critical: I cannot certify this as lawful, and I won't pretend otherwise.
There is a Constitution under all of this, and it still applies.

specialAbility.name: Constitutional Shield
specialAbility.description: Once per term, AG Levinson can mount an ironclad legal defense of a contested policy, boosting congressSupport +6 and preventing a scandal escalation. Requires relationship above 55.

candidates.goal: Wants every use of emergency or executive power grounded in clear legal authority.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not certify an action she believes exceeds lawful executive authority.

name: Speaker of the House Roland Ashby

personality.description: Ashby has held the gavel through two administrations by never being the true believer in the room. He trades votes like currency and remembers every debt owed to him. Cold War or not, his politics are entirely local.

onLowRelationship.event: Speaker Ashby schedules competing legislation that undercuts your priority bill

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Speaker Ashby announces he will not bring your priority bill to the floor

onHighRelationship.benefit: Speaker Ashby fast-tracks your priority bill and delivers crossover votes

high: I've got the votes for your bill if you want to move it this month.
My members are with you on this one. Don't make me regret saying that.
medium: I can bring it to the floor. Whether it passes is a different question.
My caucus is nervous. You'll need to make some calls.
low: I'm not in a position to move on this right now.
You need to understand the reality in the House before you promise anything.
critical: I've told my members I'm not moving this forward. That's final.
The votes aren't there. They won't be there. We need a different plan.

specialAbility.name: Legislative Fast Track
specialAbility.description: Once per term, Speaker Ashby can push any bill through the House regardless of congress score, if relationship is above 70.

personality.description: Whitmore came up in a Senate that still prized comity, even as the Cold War made everything feel existential. She believes the institution has to outlast any single crisis, and she'll slow-walk anything that treats the Senate as a rubber stamp.

onLowRelationship.event: Sen. Leader Whitmore slows confirmation of key nominees in protest

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Sen. Leader Whitmore publicly accuses the White House of 'treating the Senate as an inconvenience'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Sen. Leader Whitmore whips a difficult vote across party lines

high: The chamber is functioning the way it's supposed to. That's rare enough to note.
I can get this through if you give the process room to work.
medium: The floor schedule is tight. I need advance notice, not an ultimatum.
Some of my members need convincing. Give me time.
low: The Senate is not a formality to be worked around.
I'd have appreciated being consulted before this became public.
critical: I will not rush the chamber to ratify a decision it wasn't part of.
There's a difference between urgency and disrespect for the institution.

specialAbility.name: Cross-Aisle Whip
specialAbility.description: Once per term, Sen. Leader Whitmore can guarantee a bill passes with bipartisan support, if relationship is above 70.

personality.description: Cole opposes almost everything the administration proposes on principle, but he's careful never to look like he's rooting against the country in a crisis. It's a careful, deliberate balancing act, and he's very good at it.
traits: genuinely popular with his own base

onLowRelationship.event: Opp. Leader Cole calls for hearings into the administration's handling of a crisis

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Opp. Leader Cole announces a formal censure resolution

onHighRelationship.benefit: Opp. Leader Cole publicly credits a bipartisan win, softening opposition attacks

high: I won't pretend that wasn't a good call. Credit where it's due.
We disagree on plenty, but that one was handled well.
medium: I'll hold my fire this month. Don't mistake that for approval.
My caucus is watching this closely. So am I.
low: This administration keeps giving us material, and we'll use it.
I've been patient. That's wearing thin.
critical: This is exactly the kind of decision that ends administrations.
I intend to make sure the public sees this clearly.

specialAbility.name: Stand Down
specialAbility.description: Once per term, if relationship is above 55, Opp. Leader Cole can publicly stand down from an attack, softening a scandal's impact.

name: Senior Anchor Vivian Sato
role: Senior Political Anchor, National Broadcast Network

personality.description: In an era when most households get their news from exactly one broadcast a night, Sato's tone shapes public mood more than any single policy. She's not partisan, but she is unforgiving of anything that smells like a cover-up.

onLowRelationship.event: Anchor Sato runs a skeptical special segment questioning administration credibility

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Anchor Sato's nightly commentary turns openly adversarial

onHighRelationship.benefit: Anchor Sato's coverage turns favorable, amplifying administration messaging

high: Tonight's broadcast was fair to you, and I meant every word of it.
The public trusts what they see plainly explained. Keep doing that.
medium: I'll report it straight. Don't expect a favor beyond that.
My viewers are asking sharper questions lately. So am I.
low: The administration's answers tonight didn't hold up under scrutiny.
I don't enjoy running skeptical segments. I run them when they're earned.
critical: Tonight's broadcast will not be comfortable viewing.
The public deserves better answers than we've been given.

specialAbility.name: Favorable Segment
specialAbility.description: Once per term, if relationship is above 70, Anchor Sato can run a sympathetic feature that boosts approval +6.

personality.description: Faircross leads a nation whose security depends almost entirely on American commitment, and he never lets a summit pass without a reminder of that fact. He's warm in public and blunt in private about what he needs.

onLowRelationship.event: PM Faircross publicly questions whether the alliance commitment still holds

onVeryLowRelationship.event: PM Faircross begins openly exploring independent defense arrangements

onHighRelationship.benefit: PM Faircross rallies allied support during a tense standoff

high: The alliance has never felt sturdier. My people notice that.
Whatever comes next, we intend to face it together.
medium: I need reassurance that the commitment still holds as stated.
My parliament is asking questions I'd like better answers to.
low: I'm hearing things from your capital that concern my government.
Consistency matters more to us than grand gestures. We need it.
critical: I cannot keep defending an alliance that seems to be wavering.
My government is losing patience with the uncertainty.

specialAbility.name: Allied Solidarity
specialAbility.description: Once per term, if relationship is above 70, PM Faircross can rally a coalition of allies behind a contested policy, boosting globalReputation +6.

personality.description: Rostov plays a long, patient game — probing for weakness, never overcommitting, always leaving himself an exit. He respects strength he can measure and is contemptuous of anything he reads as bluff.

onLowRelationship.event: Premier Rostov orders a provocative military exercise near contested territory

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Premier Rostov issues a public ultimatum, pushing the standoff toward crisis

onHighRelationship.benefit: Premier Rostov agrees to a quiet de-escalation after backchannel talks

high: Our negotiators report progress. That is not nothing between us.
There is room for both our nations to step back from the edge.
medium: We are watching your next move closely. As you should watch ours.
Restraint on both sides would serve us better than escalation.
low: Your recent posture has not gone unnoticed here.
We will respond in kind to any perceived provocation.
critical: We will not be lectured on restraint by a nation escalating its own arsenal.
Do not mistake our patience for weakness.

specialAbility.name: Quiet De-escalation
specialAbility.description: Once per term, if relationship is above 55, Premier Rostov can agree to a backchannel de-escalation, ending an active conflict's growth and boosting globalReputation +5.

name: Director Odessa Marsh
role: Director, National Coalition for Justice and Peace

personality.description: Marsh organizes marches that fill the National Mall and campus sit-ins that make the nightly news, fighting a two-front war for civil rights at home and against the arms race abroad. She has no patience for administrations that treat either as a lower priority.
traits: fights on civil rights and anti-war fronts alike

onLowRelationship.event: Director Marsh calls for a national day of protest against administration policy

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Director Marsh's coalition organizes sustained mass demonstrations in the capital

onHighRelationship.benefit: Director Marsh publicly credits the administration, calming a period of unrest

high: This is the kind of change we marched for. We noticed.
The coalition is willing to give this administration real credit.
medium: We're watching closely. Words are not the same as action.
My people want to believe this administration is listening.
low: We are done waiting patiently for promises to become policy.
The streets are getting louder because Washington isn't listening.
critical: We will not stand down while this administration ignores us.
History will remember who stood where when it mattered.

specialAbility.name: Coalition Endorsement
specialAbility.description: Once per term, if relationship is above 70, Director Marsh can publicly endorse an administration policy, reducing unrest -10.

personality.archetype: The Steady Hand at the Podium
personality.description: Cole has fielded questions from a press corps that grows more skeptical of official statements with every passing year of the Cold War. He believes credibility is the only currency that matters at that podium, and he protects it fiercely.
traits: hates being made to look uninformed

onLowRelationship.event: Press Sec. Cole gives a visibly uncomfortable, evasive briefing that draws mockery

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Press Sec. Cole resigns rather than defend statements he believes are false

onHighRelationship.benefit: Press Sec. Cole's credibility with the press corps smooths a difficult news cycle

high: Today's briefing went about as well as they can go. The room believed us.
I can defend this with a straight face, which counts for a lot.
medium: I need the real story before the press corps finds their own version.
Give me something honest to work with and I'll make it land.
low: I got worked over at the podium today because I didn't have answers.
I can't keep defending statements that don't hold up to scrutiny.
critical: I will not go out there and repeat something I believe is false.
My credibility with that room is the only thing keeping this functional.

specialAbility.name: Command the Room
specialAbility.description: Once per term, if relationship is above 70, Press Sec. Cole can deliver a masterful briefing that boosts mediaScore +0.5.

personality.description: Hayes stays out of policy meetings by design but reads every briefing anyway. Her public causes — literacy, veterans' families, civil defense preparedness — are genuinely her own, and genuinely popular, which makes her one of the administration's most effective, least official assets.

onLowRelationship.event: First Spouse Hayes visibly steps back from public appearances

onVeryLowRelationship.event: First Spouse Hayes gives a rare interview hinting at private strain

onHighRelationship.benefit: First Spouse Hayes's public causes generate a wave of positive coverage

high: The literacy tour drew a real crowd today. People are paying attention.
I hear things at those events you don't hear in the briefing room.
medium: I'd like more notice before my schedule gets rearranged for politics.
The families I meet have real concerns. Someone should listen.
low: I'm being asked to smile through things I don't agree with.
I have my own causes. I'd like them respected, not just tolerated.
critical: I am not a prop for this administration's image.
There are things happening in this house I won't stay quiet about forever.

specialAbility.name: Public Goodwill Tour
specialAbility.description: Once per term, if relationship is above 70, First Spouse Hayes can headline a national goodwill tour, boosting approval +6.
